summary: Jeff Atwood explores how poorly-constructed regular expressions can cause catastrophic performance problems, triggered by a Microsoft Hotmail developer's warning about regex pitfalls during their .NET 2.0 upgrade. He demonstrates with a simple pattern (x+x+)+y that exhibits exponential time growth, showing how a 25-character string takes over 25 seconds to process. The post explains that this 'catastrophic backtracking' occurs when the regex engine tries an exponential number of futile possibilities on non-matching input. The solution is to write patterns where there's only one way to match any given string, being as specific as possible with character classes.
key_points: Regular expressions can exhibit exponential CPU time growth with each additional input character, causing severe performance bottlenecks
Catastrophic backtracking often strikes on non-matching input - a regex that works fine on expected strings can go haywire on erroneous ones
The .NET regex engine lacks built-in throttling or exception handling for runaway expressions, unlike more advanced tools like RegexBuddy
The fix is ensuring there's only one way to match any given string by using specific character classes instead of greedy wildcards
This is a real production concern - Hotmail took 'several hotfixes' for regex performance issues during their .NET upgrade
money_quotes: RegExes are so handy, but devs really need to understand how they work; we've gotten bitten by them.
It's entirely possible to formulate regular expression patterns that consume exponentially more CPU time for each additional input character.

A Regex that works well when the input matches can still go nuts trying to match a non-matching string, consuming an exponential amount of time trying an exponential number of futile backtracking possibilities.
I'm definitely guilty of this. When I throw a regex together, I never worry about performance.
Looks like n-squared behavior to me.
When nesting repetition operators, make absolutely sure that there is only one way to match the same match.
key_insight: Regular expressions can become performance time bombs not on expected input, but on malformed or non-matching strings, where backtracking engines try exponentially many futile combinations.
